_meta.purpose: Per-state window data dictionary for Phase A.2 state-vertical hub pages (10-window full-house residential window replacement focus, 1500 sq ft single-story home).
_meta.sources: ENERGY STAR Most Efficient Windows climate zones (Northern / North-Central / South-Central / Southern) and U-factor + SHGC mandates; IECC 2021 Table R402.1.3 prescriptive U-factor and SHGC by climate zone; FBC §1620 + Miami-Dade HVHZ Notice of Acceptance for impact-rated windows; ASTM E1996 large-missile impact testing standard; AAMA / WDMA / CSA 101/I.S.2/A440 NAFS performance grade testing; state contractor licensing boards verified by URL; BLS OEWS May 2024 (SOC 47-2031 Carpenters as window installer wage anchor); state historic preservation overlays (NPS National Historic Districts); state energy stretch codes (CA Title 24 Part 6, MA Stretch Code, NY Stretch Code, WA WSEC); lead-safe RRP requirement under EPA 40 CFR Part 745 for pre-1978 housing.

fields.energy_star_climate_zone: Northern / North-Central / South-Central / Southern — ENERGY STAR Most Efficient Windows mandate
fields.iecc_zone: IECC 2021 climate zone — drives U-factor + SHGC + air-leakage requirements per Table R402.1.3
fields.max_shgc: IECC 2021 prescriptive maximum Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (or 'no-limit' in coldest climate zones)
fields.design_wind_speed_mph: ASCE 7-22 mapped basic wind speed (Risk Category II, mph) — drives DASMA / NAFS performance grade selection
fields.hurricane_impact_rated_required: yes-statewide / yes-coastal-counties / no-mandate — HVHZ ASTM E1996 large-missile impact resistance
fields.dominant_frame_material: vinyl / wood / aluminum-clad-wood / fiberglass / composite-pultruded / aluminum
fields.historic_overlay_prevalence: very-high / high / moderate / low — NPS National Historic District + state-level historic preservation review
fields.lead_safe_rrp_pre_1978_housing_share: very-high / high / moderate / low — share of housing built pre-1978 driving EPA RRP lead-safe certification
fields.license_board_name: Issuing authority for residential window contractor licensing
fields.permit_typical: Typical permit threshold for residential window replacement
fields.carpenter_window_installer_wage_mean_hou: BLS OEWS May 2024 state mean hourly wage for SOC 47-2031 Carpenters (window installer anchor wage) (USD)
fields.avg_window_low: Typical low end of 10-window 1500 sq ft single-story full-house window replacement (USD)
fields.avg_window_high: Typical high end (HVHZ-NOA-rated impact-resistant + wood-clad or fiberglass premium frame + custom-color cladding) (USD)
fields.distinctive_law: Single most distinctive state-specific window mandate, HOA, license, or building-code rule
fields.climate_concern: Primary climate, wind, UV, or historic-preservation driver of window glazing and frame material selection in this state

AL.name: Alabama
AL.license_board_name: Alabama Home Builders Licensure Board (HBLB) — Limited Specialty Construction credential for window installation
AL.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for window opening modification on new construction; HBLB residential builder license required above $10,000 contract; replacement-only same-size installs typically permit-exempt; Mobile and Baldwin coastal counties require ASTM E1996 large-missile impact-rated windows on every coastal residential install per Alabama Coastal Building Code
AL.distinctive_law: Alabama Coastal Building Code (post-Hurricane-Ivan amendments) requires every Mobile and Baldwin coastal-county residential window installation to comply with ASTM E1996 large-missile impact resistance plus DASMA / AAMA NAFS performance grade rating at 130 mph design wind, and Alabama Code §34-14A makes the HBLB the only state body that licenses residential window contractors above the $10,000 threshold under a Limited Specialty Construction credential, plus Alabama Coastal Insurance Endorsement separately requires every Gulf coastal residential window-replacement insurance policy to specify impact-rated glazing for Wind Loss Mitigation premium discount eligibility.
AL.climate_concern: Alabama's combination of Gulf hurricane wind exposure across Mobile and Baldwin coastal counties driving 130 mph design wind and ASTM E1996 large-missile impact-rated window glazing on every coastal residential install, ENERGY STAR Southern climate zone driving low-SHGC (≤0.25) glazing requirement statewide, persistent Gulf Coast humidity driving rapid sealant degradation on every south-facing coastal window at 5-7 year intervals, and moderate pre-1978 housing share driving EPA RRP lead-safe certification on every Birmingham, Tuscaloosa, and Montgomery historic-neighborhood replacement drives unique Gulf-hurricane-and-Southern-climate-zone-driven glazing selection.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.license_board_name: Alaska Department of Commerce — Construction Contractors Section (Specialty: Residential Endorsement)
AK.permit_typical: borough building permit required for window opening modification; Construction Contractor with Residential Endorsement required; mandatory U-0.22 or lower window specification on every Anchorage, Fairbanks, and Juneau residential install per Alaska state energy code amendments — the highest mandatory U-factor floor in the country
AK.distinctive_law: Alaska state energy code amendments mandate U-0.22 or lower window specification on every Anchorage, Fairbanks, Juneau, and Mat-Su Borough residential installation — the most aggressive mandatory window U-factor mandate in the country to address -50°F interior winter low temperatures with triple-glazed argon or krypton-fill units standard on every Alaska residential window installation, plus Alaska Statute §18.65.082 requires every Alaska window contractor to hold a Construction Contractor with Residential Endorsement plus carry a $20,000 surety bond — the highest residential window contractor bond floor in the country.
AK.climate_concern: Alaska's combination of -50°F interior winter low temperatures across Fairbanks, North Pole, and Delta Junction driving Alaska state energy code U-0.22 maximum-U-factor mandate (the most aggressive U-factor mandate in the U.S.), triple-glazed argon or krypton-fill units standard on every Alaska residential window install, and 6+ months of below-freezing temperatures driving aggressive sealant-and-condensation-management on every interior window-jamb air-sealing detail drives extreme cold-climate-driven glazing selection.

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.license_board_name: Arizona Registrar of Contractors — license CR-65 (Glazing) or CR-31 (Carpentry) for window installation
AZ.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for window opening modification; ROC CR-65 specialty license required on contracts above $1,000; HOA architectural-committee approval mandatory in 80%+ of Phoenix, Tucson, Mesa, Chandler, and Scottsdale subdivisions
AZ.distinctive_law: Arizona Registrar of Contractors §32-1121 sets the contracting threshold at $1,000 with ROC specialty CR-65 Glazing available as a dedicated credential for window installation, and Arizona is unusual among Mountain West states in HOA architectural-control prevalence — the Foundation for Community Association Research documents 80%+ of Phoenix, Tucson, Mesa, Chandler, Gilbert, and Scottsdale residential subdivisions operate HOA architectural committees with mandatory pre-approval review on window frame color and grille pattern selection plus mandatory Sun-Belt-rated UV-stabilized topcoats on every west-facing window replacement.
AZ.climate_concern: Arizona's combination of extreme summer UV exposure across the Phoenix and Tucson basins (340+ sun days/year) driving Sun-Belt-rated UV-stabilized vinyl frames and low-SHGC (≤0.25) glazing on every west-facing residential window installation to prevent fade and minimize cooling load, ENERGY STAR Southern climate zone driving low-SHGC (≤0.25) glazing requirement statewide, and very-high HOA architectural-control prevalence across 80%+ of Phoenix and Tucson subdivisions requiring pre-approval review on window frame color drives unique Sun-Belt-and-HOA-driven glazing selection.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.license_board_name: Arkansas Contractors Licensing Board — Residential Builder license (>$2,000)
AR.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for window opening modification; Residential Builder license required above $2,000 contract; replacement-only same-size installs typically permit-exempt; tornado-shelter rough-in detailing within attached garages mandatory under Little Rock and Conway municipal codes
AR.distinctive_law: Arkansas Code §17-25-101 sets one of the lowest residential contracting thresholds at $2,000 with the Arkansas Contractors Licensing Board issuing a Residential Builder license, plus Arkansas is unusual among Tornado Alley states in not enforcing a statewide impact-resistant window mandate although individual Little Rock, Conway, Fayetteville, and Bentonville municipalities separately recommend impact-resistant glazing on every south-and-west-facing residential window installation to address 30+ tornadoes per year per NWS Little Rock.
AR.climate_concern: Arkansas's Tornado Alley exposure with NWS Little Rock documenting an average 30 tornadoes per year drives optional impact-resistant glazing recommendation on every south-and-west-facing residential window installation across Little Rock, Pine Bluff, Hot Springs, and Fayetteville, plus ENERGY STAR South-Central climate zone driving moderate U-factor (≤0.32) and SHGC (≤0.40) requirements statewide, and red-clay subsoil shrink-swell driving aggressive header-attachment detailing on every Little Rock-area residential install.

CA.name: California
CA.license_board_name: California Contractors State License Board (CSLB) — C-17 Glazing specialty
CA.permit_typical: city building permit required for window opening modification; CSLB C-17 specialty license required above $500 contract; California Title 24 Part 6 mandatory above-IECC-baseline U-factor and SHGC; CALGreen Title 24 Part 11 mandatory recycled-content review; HOA architectural committee approval mandatory in 60%+ of California subdivisions
CA.distinctive_law: California Title 24 Part 6 (California Energy Code) imposes the most aggressive prescriptive window U-factor and SHGC requirements in the country with 16 separate climate zones each with distinct U-0.30-to-U-0.32 maximum U-factors and SHGC ≤ 0.25 maximums, plus the California Contractors State License Board (CSLB) C-17 Glazing specialty license is one of the most credential-segmented residential window contractor licensing schemes in the country, and CALGreen Title 24 Part 11 separately requires recycled-content review on every window installation specifying source-reduction or recycled-content percentage of the vinyl or aluminum frame, with mandatory NFRC labeling on every California residential window install.
CA.climate_concern: California's combination of California Title 24 Part 6 climate-zone-specific U-factor and SHGC mandates driving the most stringent prescriptive window energy requirements in the country, CAL FIRE Tier 3 WUI overlay across the Sierra foothills additionally requiring tempered-or-multi-pane glazing on every WUI residential property, persistent Pacific salt-spray on every coastal exposure driving stainless-hardware-and-aluminum-clad-wood frame replacement at 7-10 year intervals, very-high HOA architectural-control prevalence across 60%+ of California subdivisions, and high pre-1978 housing share driving EPA RRP lead-safe certification on every San Francisco, Oakland, Berkeley, Los Angeles, and Sacramento historic-neighborhood window replacement drives unique CA-Title-24-and-WUI-driven glazing selection.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.license_board_name: No statewide window license — Denver, Colorado Springs, Aurora, and other home-rule municipalities issue local Building Contractor credentials
CO.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for window opening modification; no state license; replacement-only same-size installs typically permit-exempt; Colorado state energy code amendments mandate U-0.30 or lower; HOA architectural committee approval mandatory in 70%+ of Front Range subdivisions
CO.distinctive_law: Colorado Revised Statutes §38-33.3 (Colorado Common Interest Ownership Act) provides one of the most enforceable HOA architectural-control statutes in the country with mandatory pre-approval review on window frame color, grille pattern, and exterior finish across 70%+ of Front Range subdivisions in Aurora, Castle Rock, Highlands Ranch, Parker, Centennial, and Lone Tree, plus Colorado state energy code amendments separately mandate U-0.30 or lower window U-factor on every Colorado residential installation.
CO.climate_concern: Colorado's combination of high-altitude UV exposure across the Front Range driving Sun-Belt-rated UV-stabilized vinyl frames and laminated-glazing recommended on every south-and-west-facing residential window installation, ENERGY STAR Northern climate zone driving low-U-factor (≤0.30) glazing requirement statewide, F2 freeze-thaw cycling driving sealant degradation on every window perimeter caulk at 4-6 year intervals, and high HOA architectural-control prevalence across 70%+ of Front Range subdivisions drives unique high-altitude-UV-and-HOA-driven glazing selection.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.license_board_name: Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration

CT.climate_concern: Connecticut's combination of 130 mph ASCE 7-22 design wind speed across Atlantic coastal counties driving ASTM E1996 large-missile impact-rated window mandate on every coastal residential install, persistent Long Island Sound salt-spray on every Fairfield, New Haven, Middlesex, and New London county exposure, very-high pre-1978 housing share (60%+ across the state, the highest in the country) driving mandatory EPA RRP lead-safe certification on every Connecticut residential window replacement, and ENERGY STAR Northern climate zone U-0.30 mandate drives unique coastal-and-historic-housing-driven glazing selection.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.license_board_name: Delaware Division of Revenue — Business License (window installer)
DE.permit_typical: county building permit required for window opening modification; Delaware Business License only — no state-issued specialty license; replacement-only same-size installs typically permit-exempt; Sussex County coastal counties mandate ASTM E1996 large-missile impact-rated windows
DE.distinctive_law: Delaware is one of the few East Coast states with no specialty window contractor licensing — every Delaware window contractor operates under a Delaware Division of Revenue Business License only, with no examination, bond, or board oversight, leaving Sussex County coastal property owners to rely on contractor BBB ratings rather than a state credential check, and Delaware Sussex County coastal jurisdictions separately require ASTM E1996 large-missile impact resistance on every coastal residential window installation to address Atlantic hurricane exposure with 130 mph ASCE 7-22 design wind speed.
DE.climate_concern: Delaware's combination of 130 mph ASCE 7-22 design wind speed across Sussex County and southern Kent County driving ASTM E1996 large-missile impact-rated window mandate on every coastal residential install, persistent Atlantic and Delaware Bay salt-spray on every Sussex County coastal exposure driving aluminum-clad-wood frame recommended on every coastal install, FEMA NFIP base-flood-elevation foundation height verification on every Bethany Beach to Rehoboth coastal residential install, and ENERGY STAR North-Central climate zone U-0.32 mandate drives unique coastal-Atlantic-and-North-Central-climate-driven glazing selection.

FL.name: Florida
FL.license_board_name: Florida DBPR Construction Industry Licensing Board — Specialty Glass and Glazing Contractor (SCC131151) or Certified Building Contractor
FL.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for every window installation in Miami-Dade and Broward counties (HVHZ); Florida Product Approval (FL #) NOA verification mandatory on every HVHZ install; FL DBPR Specialty Glass and Glazing Contractor license required statewide; impact-resistant glazing mandatory on every coastal-county residential install
FL.distinctive_law: Florida Building Code §1620 + ASTM E1996 imposes the most restrictive coastal hurricane-zone window requirements in the country — every HVHZ residential window installation in Miami-Dade and Broward counties requires Florida Product Approval (FL #) compliance with Notice of Acceptance (NOA) testing for hurricane wind uplift up to 175 mph plus large-missile impact resistance per ASTM E1996 (D-rated 9-lb 2x4 impact at 50 fps), and FL DBPR separately licenses a Specialty Glass and Glazing Contractor (SCC131151) with mandatory examination on Florida Building Code wind-pressure calculations, FBC 1626 large-missile impact testing, and HVHZ NOA submittal documentation — the only dedicated state-level glazing specialty license for HVHZ work in the U.S., plus FL Statute §553.844 Wind Loss Mitigation insurance discount eligibility for impact-rated glazing on every Florida residential install.
FL.climate_concern: Florida's HVHZ Miami-Dade and Broward 175 mph design wind speed plus large-missile impact resistance requirement under FBC §1620 + ASTM E1996 D-rated drives the most expensive residential window installation requirements in the country at $15,000-$45,000 for full-house Miami-Dade NOA-rated D-rated impact-resistant residential windows, plus statewide impact-resistant requirement on every coastal-county installation, ENERGY STAR Southern climate zone driving low-SHGC (≤0.25) glazing requirement statewide, persistent Atlantic and Gulf salt-spray driving aluminum-clad-wood or fiberglass frame replacement at 5-7 year intervals, and FL Statute §553.844 Wind Loss Mitigation insurance discount eligibility for impact-rated glazing drives unique HVHZ-and-Wind-Loss-Mitigation-driven glazing selection.

GA.climate_concern: Georgia's combination of Atlantic hurricane wind exposure across coastal counties (Chatham, Bryan, Liberty, McIntosh, Glynn, Camden) driving ASTM E1996 large-missile impact-rated window mandate on every coastal residential install, ENERGY STAR South-Central climate zone driving low-SHGC (≤0.25) and U-factor (≤0.32) requirement statewide, persistent Gulf-Atlantic humidity driving rapid sealant degradation on every south-facing window at 5-7 year intervals, and high HOA architectural-control prevalence across Atlanta-metro subdivisions drives moderate hurricane-and-Southern-climate-zone-driven glazing selection.

HI.permit_typical: county building permit required for window opening modification; HI DCCA C-22a or C-22b license required statewide; mandatory hurricane-rated post-Iniki impact-resistant glazing on every Kauai residential window installation; mandatory anti-corrosion stainless-hardware specification on every coastal residential install

ID.name: Idaho
ID.license_board_name: Idaho Bureau of Occupational Licenses — Public Works Contractor License (PWCL) for residential >$2,000 + Idaho Contractors Registration
ID.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for window opening modification; PWCL required for residential work above $2,000; replacement-only same-size installs typically permit-exempt; Treasure Valley HOA architectural-committee approval common on Boise-Meridian-Nampa subdivisions
